title: 7 Herbal Creams For Ulcerative Colitis
intro_desc: If you're living with ulcerative colitis, you know how painful and uncomfortable it can be. One natural way to find some relief is by using herbal creams. These creams are made from plant extracts that have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce the swelling and discomfort in your digestive tract. One example of an herb that can help is Curcuma longa, also known as turmeric. Curcuma longa contains a compound called curcumin, which has powerful anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce pain and inflammation in the gut. Another herb that can help is Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger. Ginger has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce nausea and vomiting, which are common symptoms of ulcerative colitis. Aloe barbadensis, also known as aloe vera, is another herb that can help soothe and calm the digestive tract. When applied topically as a cream, aloe vera can help reduce inflammation and irritation in the gut, providing quick relief from symptoms. By using herbal creams, you can find some relief from the discomfort and pain of ulcerative colitis, and start to enjoy a better quality of life.

remedies.remedy_desc: Curcuma longa creams contains curcumin, which is a bioactive constituent that has anti-inflammatory properties. It also contains other compounds like demethoxycurcumin and bisdemethoxycurcumin that help reduce inflammation in the body. These properties are beneficial for people with ulcerative colitis because they can help decrease the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, which contribute to the condition. The antioxidant properties of curcumin also help protect the colon from damage caused by free radicals. By reducing inflammation and protecting the colon, Curcuma longa creams may help alleviate symptoms of ulcerative colitis, such as diarrhea and abdominal pain.
remedies.image_alt: Curcuma longa herbal creams for ulcerative colitis
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, 1 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of turmeric powder, 2 tablespoons of coconut oil for infusion.
Infuse coconut oil with turmeric: Heat 2 tablespoons of coconut oil in a small saucepan over low heat. Add 1 teaspoon of turmeric powder. Remove from heat and let it sit for 2 hours.
Melt ingredients: In a double boiler, melt 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, and 1 cup of beeswax. Stir until smooth and combined.
Add infused coconut oil and essential oil: Add the infused coconut oil and 10 drops of tea tree oil to the melted mixture. Stir well.
Pour into a mold: Pour the mixture into a mold or a jar. Let it cool and harden. Once set, your Curcuma longa cream for ulcerative colitis is ready to use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale creams contains active constituents like gingerol and shogaol, which have potent anti-inflammatory properties. These compounds help reduce inflammation and swelling in the digestive tract, a common issue for people with ulcerative colitis. Ginger's antispasmodic properties can also help alleviate cramps and spasms in the intestines, providing relief from symptoms. Additionally, ginger has antioxidant properties that can help protect the gut from damage caused by free radicals. By reducing inflammation and protecting the gut, Zingiber officinale creams may be a useful complementary treatment for managing ulcerative colitis symptoms.
remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal creams for ulcerative colitis
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 2 cups of coconut oil, 1 cup of shea butter, 1/2 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of ginger oil, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil, and 2 tablespoons of aloe vera gel.
Melt the coconut oil, shea butter, and beeswax in a double boiler over low heat, stirring occasionally.
Once melted, remove from heat and let cool slightly. Stir in the ginger oil, vitamin E oil, and aloe vera gel.
Pour the mixture into small containers and let cool completely. This will help the mixture harden and become a solid cream.
Store the Zingiber officinale cream in the fridge for up to 2 weeks. Before use, gently massage the cream onto the affected area to help soothe and calm ulcerative colitis symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Aloe barbadensis creams contains bioactive constituents like aloin, aloe-emodin, and acemannan. These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce inflammation in the colon, a common issue for people with ulcerative colitis. The anti-inflammatory properties also help reduce the severity of symptoms like diarrhea and abdominal pain. Aloe-emodin has been shown to inhibit the growth of bacteria that can cause inflammation, which contributes to the development of ulcerative colitis. The soothing properties of acemannan help repair the lining of the colon, promoting a healthy environment for the growth of good bacteria.
remedies.image_alt: Aloe barbadensis herbal creams for ulcerative colitis
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of distilled water, 1/4 cup of aloe vera gel, 2 tablespoons of coconut oil, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, 2 teaspoons of vitamin E oil, and 2 teaspoons of honey.
In a saucepan, heat the distilled water over low heat. Add the aloe vera gel, coconut oil, and shea butter. Stir until the mixture is well combined.
Remove the saucepan from the heat and let it cool for 10 minutes. Add the vitamin E oil and honey. Stir well until the mixture is smooth.
Strain the mixture into a clean bowl to remove any impurities. Let it cool and thicken for 30 minutes.
Transfer the mixture into a container and refrigerate it for at least 2 hours. Once set, scoop out the cream and apply it to the affected areas as needed.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra creams contains licorice root extract, which has been found to help with ulcerative colitis. The active constituents, glycyrrhetinic acid and glabridin, have anti-inflammatory properties that reduce inflammation in the colon. Glycyrrhizin in licorice root also acts as an antioxidant, preventing damage to the colon lining and promoting healing. Additionally, licorice root extract helps to soothe and protect the mucous membranes in the colon, reducing symptoms of ulcerative colitis such as bleeding and diarrhea. By reducing inflammation and promoting healing, Glycyrrhiza glabra creams can help manage symptoms of ulcerative colitis.
remedies.image_alt: Glycyrrhiza glabra herbal creams for ulcerative colitis
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of distilled water, 2 tablespoons of glycerin, and 4 tablespoons of dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root powder.
Boil the distilled water in a saucepan, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 10 minutes.
Add the dried Glycyrrhiza glabra root powder to the simmering water and let it steep for 20 minutes.
Strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve into a clean bowl. Discard the solids.
Mix 2 tablespoons of the strained liquid with 2 tablespoons of glycerin and 1 tablespoon of a carrier oil (like coconut oil) to create the cream. Store in an airtight container in the fridge.

remedies.remedy_desc: Echinacea purpurea creams contains bioactive constituents like alkylamides, phenolic acids, and glycoproteins. These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and irritation in the digestive tract. The flavonoids present in Echinacea purpurea also have antioxidant properties, which can protect the gut from damage caused by free radicals. Additionally, the polysaccharides in Echinacea purpurea can stimulate the immune system, which may help regulate the body's response to inflammation in the digestive tract. By reducing inflammation and promoting a healthy gut environment, Echinacea purpurea creams may provide relief for individuals with ulcerative colitis.
remedies.image_alt: Echinacea purpurea herbal creams for ulcerative colitis
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 1 cup of dried Echinacea purpurea flowers, 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil, and 2 tablespoons of lavender oil.
Melt coconut oil and beeswax in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl over a pot of simmering water.
Add dried Echinacea purpurea flowers to the melted coconut oil and beeswax mixture. Let it steep for 10-15 minutes, then strain the mixture through a cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve.
Add vitamin E oil and lavender oil to the strained mixture. Stir well to combine.
Pour the mixture into small containers or tin cans. Let it cool and solidify. Store in a cool, dry place.

remedies.remedy_desc: Terminalia chebula creams contains bioactive constituents like chebulic acid, chebulinic acid, and flavonoids. These compounds have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce inflammation in the digestive tract associated with ulcerative colitis. Chebulic acid also has antioxidant properties, which can protect the colon lining from damage and promote healing. Flavonoids in Terminalia chebula creams may also help reduce oxidative stress and improve the integrity of the gut lining. By reducing inflammation and promoting healing, Terminalia chebula creams may help alleviate symptoms of ulcerative colitis.
remedies.image_alt: Terminalia chebula herbal creams for ulcerative colitis
remedy_procedure: Gather ingredients: 100g of Terminalia chebula powder, 100g of coconut oil, 100g of shea butter, 2 tablespoons of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil, and a small cup of distilled water.
Melt the coconut oil and shea butter in a double boiler or a heat-proof bowl set over a pot of simmering water. Stir until smooth.
Add the Terminalia chebula powder to the melted mixture and stir well. Let it sit for 10 minutes to allow the powder to infuse.
Add the beeswax, vitamin E oil, and distilled water to the mixture. Stir until the beeswax is fully incorporated and the mixture is smooth.
Pour the mixture into a clean, sterilized jar and let it cool and harden. Once hardened, your Terminalia chebula cream is ready to use.

remedies.remedy_desc: Calendula officinalis creams contains triterpenoids, flavonoids, and carotenoids as its bioactive constituents. These compounds have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties that can help reduce inflammation and promote healing in the digestive tract. The triterpenoids in Calendula officinalis cream, such as saponins and sesquiterpenes, have been shown to inhibit the production of inflammatory chemicals, which can contribute to ulcerative colitis symptoms. The flavonoids, like quercetin and luteolin, have antioxidant properties that can help protect the colon from damage caused by free radicals. By reducing inflammation and promoting healing, Calendula officinalis cream may help alleviate symptoms of ulcerative colitis, such as diarrhea, abdominal pain, and rectal bleeding.
remedies.image_alt: Calendula officinalis herbal creams for ulcerative colitis
remedy_procedure: Gather 1 cup of Calendula officinalis flowers, 1/2 cup of coconut oil, 1/4 cup of beeswax, 2 tablespoons of shea butter, and 2 tablespoons of vitamin E oil.
Infuse the Calendula flowers in the coconut oil by placing them in a clean glass jar and letting it sit in a cool, dark place for 2-3 weeks, shaking the jar daily.
Strain the coconut oil through a cheesecloth or a coffee filter into a clean bowl, discarding the Calendula flowers.
In a double boiler, melt the beeswax and shea butter. Add the infused coconut oil and vitamin E oil, and stir until well combined.
Pour the mixture into small containers and let it cool and solidify. Store the cream in the fridge for up to 2 weeks.

faq_prevent: Drinking herbal tea may help prevent ulcerative colitis from forming, but more research is needed to confirm this. Some herbal teas, like ginger and turmeric, have anti-inflammatory properties that could help soothe the gut. However, the effects of these teas on ulcerative colitis are still unknown and need further study to understand their potential benefits.
faq_safe: Consuming herbal teas for ulcerative colitis every day can be a bit tricky. While some teas, like peppermint and chamomile, may help calm digestive issues, others might not be as effective. Some herbal teas may even interact with medications or worsen symptoms. It's essential to be cautious and monitor your body's response.
faq_timeline: Herbal teas can help manage ulcerative colitis symptoms, but it's hard to say exactly when results will show. Some people may notice a difference in a few days, while others might take a week or two. It depends on the individual and the specific herbal tea being used.
faq_time: For ulcerative colitis, drinking herbal tea in the morning may help ease symptoms. A warm cup can help stimulate digestion and calm the gut. Many people find that sipping herbal tea first thing in the day helps set a gentle pace for their digestive system.
faq_interaction: Herbal teas can interact with prescription medications for ulcerative colitis. Some herbs, like St. John's Wort and ginkgo biloba, can speed up or slow down how your body absorbs certain medicines. This might affect how well they work or increase the risk of side effects. If you drink herbal teas, it's essential to know the potential interactions with your medications.
faq_pregnant: If you have ulcerative colitis and are pregnant, it's best to talk to someone about your herbal tea choices. Some herbal teas, like peppermint and chamomile, can help with colitis symptoms, but others might not be safe during pregnancy. For example, pennyroyal tea can cause birth defects. Be careful with what you drink.
faq_breastfeeding: Herbal teas for ulcerative colitis can be a good choice, but it's best to be cautious while breastfeeding. Some herbal teas, like peppermint and chamomile, are usually safe, but others, like ginger and licorice root, may pass into breast milk and affect the baby. Always check the ingredients and talk to your loved ones if you have any concerns.
title_2: How to Heal Ulcerative Colitis with 7 Youth-Friendly Herbal Creams
